items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: What does Kant's categorical imperative require?
items.correct: Acting only on maxims you could will to be universal laws, and treating people as ends in themselves, never merely as means.
items.misconception: Treat others how you would like to be treated.
items.random_wrong: Do whatever your conscience tells you to do.
items.why: The Golden Rule depends on your personal preferences (Kant explicitly rejected it). The categorical imperative tests universalisability — whether the maxim can coherently be a law for everyone.

items.q: What is the focus of virtue ethics?
items.correct: On character and habits — becoming the kind of person who acts well, aiming at eudaimonia (flourishing) through practising virtues that lie between extremes.
items.misconception: Virtue ethics tells you which specific actions are right and wrong.
items.random_wrong: Virtue ethics is about following the Ten Commandments.
items.why: Virtue ethics asks 'what kind of person should I be?' rather than 'what should I do?'. Students try to extract action-rules from it, missing its character-based agent-focused nature.

items.q: Does emotivism claim that moral statements are false?
items.correct: No — emotivism claims they are not truth-apt at all: 'stealing is wrong' expresses disapproval rather than stating anything true or false.
items.misconception: Emotivism says all moral statements are false because morality is fake.
items.random_wrong: Emotivism says moral statements are true when most people agree with them.
items.why: The view that moral claims are systematically false is error theory (Mackie), not emotivism. Distinguishing non-cognitivism from error theory is a precise point that separates top answers.

items.q: Are Moore's naturalistic fallacy and Hume's is-ought gap the same point?
items.correct: They are related but distinct: Hume targets deriving 'ought' from 'is'; Moore targets defining 'good' as any natural property, using the open question argument.
items.misconception: Moore's naturalistic fallacy is just a renaming of Hume's is-ought gap.
items.random_wrong: Hume's is-ought gap proves that goodness is a natural property.
items.why: Moore's complaint is about definition (semantic identity), Hume's about inference across statement-types. Treating them as interchangeable blurs two separate weapons against ethical naturalism.
The answer examiners want is: Ethical naturalism. “Intuitionism” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which thinker proposed seven prima facie duties?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: W. D. Ross. “G. E. Moore” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Moore's Principia Ethica published?
items.why: Exact answer: 1903. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
